title: Can Red Lobster Stay Afloat? Inside the Turnaround Fight
content:
In the modern geopolitical landscape, infrastructure has become the ultimate battleground for global power. Ports, logistics corridors, energy systems, telecommunications networks, and subsea cables are no longer viewed simply as neutral commercial assets. Instead, they are powerful instruments of leverage used by nations to project influence and secure strategic advantages. The United States cannot credibly promote nearshoring or supply chain resilience if it allows critical infrastructure in its own hemisphere to be shaped by competitors. Permitting opaque financing and weak governance to dictate regional development creates a dangerous political dependency.

These vulnerable conditions actively undermine reliable access to essential strategic inputs like lithium, copper, and rare earths. Without these vital materials, advanced manufacturing and clean energy transitions within the United States would grind to a halt. Recognizing this threat, leaders have stressed that hemispheric competition does not offer easy off-ramps for either side. The European Union and NATO members have already unlocked significant resources and ratcheted up defense spending in response to similar challenges. Western allies are waking up to the reality that economic infrastructure is indistinguishable from national security.

However, historical missteps have taught policymakers to be cautious about overextending their reach. The famous 'Pottery Barn rule'—you break it, you own it—was meant as a deterrent against unwise foreign adventurism. Recent efforts to assume sweeping control of foreign lands have failed to instill stability, sapping billions of dollars and destroying untold goodwill. No one wants Washington to repeat these costly mistakes by acting unilaterally. Thus, the new strategy must focus on securing infrastructure through smart alliances rather than direct, heavy-handed control.
